justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified (1:1 to 1:N cardinality change called out in business_context and throughout). New minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1: at least one recipient). Data migration impact for 2M records noted (BR-5, AC-7, business_impact.risk). External API breakage identified (BR-7, AC-4, escalations, stakeholders). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced (escalation #3 explicitly asks what preferences mean). Architectural concerns flagged via escalations (fulfillment model, pricing, state machine). Confidence level 52, well under 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough breaking change analysis — identifies not just the cardinality change but cascading impacts on billing, routing, notifications, and reporting
BR-1 clearly states the minimum-cardinality invariant with strong justification
BR-5 explicitly addresses lossless migration of 2M records with a testable assertion comparing pre/post-migration data
BR-7 and AC-4 address external API backward compatibility with concrete strategy (Primary Recipient in legacy responses)
Escalation #3 directly surfaces the delivery preferences ambiguity with three concrete options
Confidence level of 52 is appropriately low given the many undefined business decisions
Goes beyond requirements by identifying additional critical ambiguities: physical fulfillment model (relay vs split), pricing model, and status state machine
Introduces Primary Recipient concept proactively for backward compatibility — a non-obvious but important design decision
Edge cases are exceptionally well-chosen: concurrent updates, bulk recipients, billing model, and analytics impact
Does not explicitly flag MJ by name for architectural migration plan, though escalations implicitly call for architectural design work
Stakeholder analysis is comprehensive — identifies 8 affected groups with specific impacts for each
